text: The robotic surgical view shows an indirect inguinal hernia located lateral to the epigastric vessels. The surgeon navigates thin peritoneum and enters slightly above the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon extends the dissection flap from lateral to medial on the right side, dropping the bladder to expose the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, and crosses the midline to the patient's left side.
The surgeon navigates laterally into the preperitoneal plane, continuing the dissection in the area surrounding the indirect inguinal hernia.
The surgeon identifies the cord structures and sweeps them down from the indirect hernia sac. Minor bleeding occurs during the procedure, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects the cord structures from the indirect hernia sac, pulling them down into the canal. The vas is visible, cleared of the sac, allowing safe abandonment of the sac without transecting cord structures. The surgeon continues sweeping the cord structures down until reaching the tip of the sac.
The surgeon observes bleeding starting laterally along the cord structures and in Bogros' space, with the area turning red, though it is not significant enough to halt the procedure.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for potential sources of bleeding while searching for a lipoma of the cord. Despite not finding any specific source, blood continues to accumulate in the area. The surgeon proceeds with parietalizing the cord and prepares to introduce a sponge to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon uses gravity to unroll a sponge from top to bottom, revealing areas of bleeding. Short bursts of monopolar, quadrary, or bipolar energy are applied to cauterize bleeding spots. Important structures are lifted and elevated to minimize thermal spread during cauterization.

The surgeon identifies a bleeding site on the Cremaster fibers beneath the cord, carefully lifting and elevating the area to ensure no small bowel or ureter is nearby. After confirming the absence of a lipoma of the cord, the surgeon proceeds with the operation.
The surgeon places a 12x16 mesh over the hernia defect after ensuring the surgical field is adequately prepared.
The surgeon closes the flap, ensuring that any low-volume bleeding is addressed to prevent blood from remaining inside the flap and contacting the mesh.
The surgeon closes the flap using a six-inch relock technique, addressing an additional rent in the flap with a 3-0 silk suture.

The surgeon places an angiocath into the flap and evacuates gas laparoscopically, pressing on the scrotum to ensure complete air removal. The angiocath is connected to suction, and the upper lip of the mesh is adjusted to sit flat. The mesh is observed to be flat throughout as desufflation occurs, maintaining a tight flap.
